chunks.text: The ice sheet of the Arctic is rapidly melting.
The evidence shown by satellite photos is shocking.
What was once white is giving way to blue ocean, one area after another.
Scientists had not predicted such rapid change.
Ten years ago
models had estimated that the kind of ice retreat we see now would happen around 2050.
But reality is advancing at a pace far beyond the prediction.
The primarily cause is the emission of greenhouse gases from human activity.
If the ice sheet melts, sea levels rise.
Small Pacific island nations such as Tuvalu and Kiribati are already facing the crisis of being submerged.
Their inhabitants are being pushed toward abandonment.
The Arctic has its own unique ecosystem — polar bears, Arctic foxes, seals.
If the ice disappears, these animals lose their habitat and face the threat of extinction.
Even more serious is the release of vast amounts of methane gas that had been trapped in the permafrost.
This brings multiple times the greenhouse effect and accelerates warming.
Governments have set up commission after commission, and commissioner debate countermeasures.
But reaching agreement to contain the problem is not easy.
The Earth is the home of all of us.
It is not someone else's problem.
If we do not act now
what will be destroyed is the dwelling of future generations.
